eli3: A 95% confidence interval (CI) does NOT mean there is a 95% probability the true parameter lies inside a particular computed interval. The true parameter is fixed (not random); it either is or isn't in the interval. The correct interpretation: if you repeated this procedure on many independent samples, 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true parameter.

The CI is built by inverting a hypothesis test. For a two-sided test of H₀: μ = μ₀ at level α, the set of all μ₀ values not rejected is exactly the (1−α) CI. This duality means a 95% CI excludes exactly the values that would be rejected at α = 0.05.

Wider CIs mean more uncertainty (smaller sample, higher variability, or higher confidence level). The z-interval [x̄ ± z_{α/2} σ/√n] uses known σ; the t-interval [x̄ ± t_{n−1, α/2} s/√n] uses estimated σ and has heavier-tailed critical values — especially important for small n.

whenWhy.title: Reporting uncertainty in parameter estimates
whenWhy.body: CIs are the primary tool for communicating estimation uncertainty. A point estimate alone (e.g., 'the effect is 3.2 units') gives no sense of precision. A CI ('3.2 ± 1.1 units, 95% CI [2.1, 4.3]') immediately conveys how confident you are. Journals and reporting guidelines (APA, AMA) require CIs alongside effect estimates.

whenWhy.title: Equivalence to hypothesis testing
whenWhy.body: A parameter value θ₀ is rejected at level α if and only if it lies outside the (1−α) CI. This duality is exact for standard tests. Use CIs instead of p-values when you care about effect magnitude, not just whether an effect is nonzero. A CI that excludes 0 corresponds to a significant test, but also tells you the plausible range.

whenWhy.title: Proportion CIs: Wilson over Wald
whenWhy.body: For binary outcomes (proportion p̂ = k/n), the standard Wald CI [p̂ ± z_{α/2}√(p̂(1−p̂)/n)] has poor coverage when p is near 0 or 1, or n is small. The Wilson (score) interval has much better coverage and should be the default. Use exact (Clopper-Pearson) CI when exact coverage is critical.

whenWhy.title: Bootstrap CIs for complex estimators
whenWhy.body: When the sampling distribution of an estimator is analytically intractable (median, correlation, model predictions), bootstrap CIs provide a nonparametric alternative. The BCa (bias-corrected and accelerated) bootstrap CI has better coverage properties than the naive percentile interval.

deepDiveIntro: A confidence interval is a random interval constructed from the data such that the probability of it containing the true parameter equals the stated confidence level, for all possible values of the parameter. The key point: the interval is random (it varies across samples) while the true parameter is fixed. Coverage probability is a property of the procedure before data is collected, not a probability statement about any specific interval after data is observed.

The derivation uses a pivot — a function of the data and the parameter whose distribution does not depend on the unknown parameter. For a normal sample with known variance, the standardized sample mean is normally distributed with mean zero and variance one regardless of the true mean. Inverting this pivot — finding all parameter values consistent with the observed data — yields the confidence interval.

deepDive.heading: z-interval (known σ)
deepDive.body: When the population standard deviation is known and the data is normally distributed (or n is large enough for the CLT), the sample mean standardized by the known standard error follows a standard normal distribution. The 95% confidence interval is the sample mean plus or minus 1.96 standard errors. To halve the width of the interval you must quadruple the sample size, because the standard error shrinks only as fast as the square root of n. [1]

deepDive.heading: t-interval (unknown σ)
deepDive.body: When the population standard deviation must be estimated from the data, the standardized sample mean follows a t-distribution with n−1 degrees of freedom rather than a standard normal. For small samples (n = 10), the t critical value of 2.26 is noticeably larger than the normal critical value of 1.96, producing a wider and more honest interval. For n greater than 30 the two distributions are nearly identical. The t-interval is exact for normal data and approximately valid for large samples from any distribution. [1]

deepDive.heading: Wilson CI for Proportions
deepDive.body: The standard Wald interval for a proportion can have actual coverage well below the nominal level when p is near 0 or 1. The Wilson interval inverts the score test statistic and adjusts the center slightly toward 0.5. It maintains near-nominal coverage across all values of p and moderate sample sizes. For binary data, Wilson is the default recommendation over the simpler but less reliable Wald interval. [1]

deepDive.heading: Common Misconceptions
deepDive.body: The most common mistake is saying 'there is a 95% probability that the true mean lies in [2.1, 4.3].' The true mean is fixed, so this statement has no frequentist meaning. The 95% refers to the procedure generating many intervals, not the probability content of any one realized interval. If you want a direct probability statement about the parameter, you need a Bayesian credible interval, which requires a prior distribution on the parameter. [2]

takeaways: 95% CI: if repeated, 95% of such intervals would contain θ. NOT a probability about θ being in this specific interval. [1]
z-interval (σ known): x̄ ± z_{α/2}·σ/√n; t-interval (σ unknown): x̄ ± t_{n−1,α/2}·s/√n. [1]
CI width ∝ 1/√n — quadruple n to halve the width. [1]
For proportions, Wilson (score) CI outperforms Wald CI, especially near p = 0 or 1. [1]
Duality: θ₀ is outside the (1−α) CI ↔ H₀: θ = θ₀ is rejected at significance level α. [1]

edgeCases.q: Can a 95% CI contain values with p-value > 0.05?
edgeCases.a: No — the duality is exact for standard tests. Every value inside the (1−α) CI corresponds to a hypothesis not rejected at level α; every value outside is rejected. However, this duality only holds for the same test statistic used to construct both. If you compute a t-interval but evaluate significance via a different test (e.g., permutation test), the duality may not hold exactly. [1]

edgeCases.q: What does 'confidence level' mean for a single computed interval?
edgeCases.a: Once you observe data and compute a specific interval [2.1, 4.3], the frequentist framework gives no probability statement about that specific interval — the unknown μ either is or isn't in it, and we cannot know. The 95% refers to the procedure, not the realization. If you want a probability statement ('P(μ ∈ [2.1, 4.3] | data) = 0.95'), you need a Bayesian credible interval with a prior on μ. [1]

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 9
Neyman (1937) — Outline of a Theory of Statistical Estimation, Phil. Trans. Royal Soc. A 236
Wilson (1927) — Probable Inference, the Law of Succession, and Statistical Inference, JASA 22(158)
